{{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[Kinai region]] and the [[Korean Peninsula]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], and [[Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[Empress Jito]]. During the [[Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[Asakura clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[JR West]] [[Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from {{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[Kinai region]] and the [[Korean Peninsula]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], and [[Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[Empress Jito]]. During the [[Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[Asakura clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[JR West]] [[Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[JR West]] [[Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese {{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[Kinai region]] and the [[Korean Peninsula]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], and [[Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[Empress Jito]]. During the [[Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[Asakura clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[JR West]] [[Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese 氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[Kinai region]] and the [[Korean Peninsula]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], and [[Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[Empress Jito]]. During the [[Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[Asakura clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[JR West]] [[Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese {{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[Kinai region]] and the [[Korean Peninsula]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], and [[Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[Empress Jito]]. During the [[Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[Asakura clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[JR West]] [[Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese {{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[Kinai region]] and the [[Korean Peninsula]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], and [[Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[Empress Jito]]. During the [[Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[Asakura clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[JR West]] [[Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Kehi Jingū]][[en:Kehi Jingū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:qq 2]] {{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[Kinai region]] and the [[Korean Peninsula]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], and [[Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[Empress Jito]]. During the [[Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[Asakura clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[JR West]] [[Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese {{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[Kinai region]] and the [[Korean Peninsula]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], and [[Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[Empress Jito]]. During the [[Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[Asakura clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[JR West]] [[Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese revision.text:
{{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[Kinai region]] and the [[Korean Peninsula]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], and [[Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[Empress Jito]]. During the [[Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[Asakura clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[JR West]] [[Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Kehi Jingū]][[en:Kehi Jingū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:qq 2]] {{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[Kinai region]] and the [[Korean Peninsula]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], and [[Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[Empress Jito]]. During the [[Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[Asakura clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[JR West]] [[Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Kehi Jingū]][[en:Kehi Jingū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:qq 2]] Jingū]][[Category|Kehi-jingu}} * {{Official|http://kehijingu.jp}} *[http://www.turuga.org/en/kehijingu/kehijingu.html Tsuruga Tourism Association] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[Kinai region]] and the [[Korean Peninsula]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], and [[Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[Empress Jito]]. During the [[Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[Asakura clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[JR West]] [[Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from
