{{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Echizen Province|Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] and the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], and [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[:en:Empress Jito|Empress Jito]]. During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its Ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[:en:Nihon Kiryaku|Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[:en:Sado Province|Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Ōno Domain|Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[:en:Matsuo Basho|Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[:en:Haiku|Haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st Rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Hokuriku Main Line|Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[:en:Arisugawa Takehito|Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Jingu|List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:11]] [[Category|Kehi-jingu}} * {{Official|http://kehijingu.jp}} *[http://www.turuga.org/en/kehijingu/kehijingu.html Tsuruga Tourism Association] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Tsuruga, Fukui]] [[Category:Echizen Province]] [[Category:8th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] }} [[sn:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingu]] [[sn:ehi Jingū]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[:en:Arisugawa Takehito|Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Jingu|List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:11]] [[Category|Kehi-jingu}} * {{Official|http://kehijingu.jp}} *[http://www.turuga.org/en/kehijingu/kehijingu.html Tsuruga Tourism Association] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Tsuruga, Fukui]] [[Category:Echizen Province]] [[Category:8th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] }} [[sn:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingu]] [[sn:ehi Jingū]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Echizen Province|Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] and the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], and [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[:en:Three Kingdoms of Korea|Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[:en:Empress Jito|Empress Jito]]. During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its Ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[:en:Nihon Kiryaku|Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[:en:Sado Province|Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]] during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[:en:Yūki Hideyasu|Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Ōno Domain|Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[:en:Matsuo Basho|Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[:en:Haiku|Haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st Rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Hokuriku Main Line|Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[:en:Arisugawa Takehito|Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Jingu|List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:11]] [[Category|Kehi-jingu}} * {{Official|http://kehijingu.jp}} *[http://www.turuga.org/en/kehijingu/kehijingu.html Tsuruga Tourism Association] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Tsuruga, Fukui]] [[Category:Echizen Province]] [[Category:8th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] }} [[sn:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingu]] [[sn:ehi Jingū]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[:en:Matsuo Basho|Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[:en:Haiku|Haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st Rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Hokuriku Main Line|Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[:en:Arisugawa Takehito|Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Jingu|List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:11]] [[Category|Kehi-jingu}} * {{Official|http://kehijingu.jp}} *[http://www.turuga.org/en/kehijingu/kehijingu.html Tsuruga Tourism Association] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Tsuruga, Fukui]] [[Category:Echizen Province]] [[Category:8th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] }} [[sn:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingu]] [[sn:ehi Jingū]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Echizen Province|Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] and the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], and [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[:en:Three Kingdoms of Korea|Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[:en:Empress Jito|Empress Jito]]. During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its Ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[:en:Nihon Kiryaku|Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[:en:Sado Province|Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]] during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[:en:Yūki Hideyasu|Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Ōno Domain|Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[:en:Matsuo Basho|Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[:en:Haiku|Haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st Rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Tsuruga Station]]on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Hokuriku Main Line|Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[:en:Arisugawa Takehito|Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Jingu|List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:11]] [[Category|Kehi-jingu}} * {{Official|http://kehijingu.jp}} *[http://www.turuga.org/en/kehijingu/kehijingu.html Tsuruga Tourism Association] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Tsuruga, Fukui]] [[Category:Echizen Province]] [[Category:8th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] }} [[sn:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingu]] [[sn:ehi Jingū]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] and the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], and [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[:en:Three Kingdoms of Korea|Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[:en:Empress Jito|Empress Jito]]. During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its Ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[:en:Nihon Kiryaku|Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[:en:Sado Province|Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]] during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[:en:Yūki Hideyasu|Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Ōno Domain|Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[:en:Matsuo Basho|Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[:en:Haiku|Haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st Rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]]on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Hokuriku Main Line|Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[:en:Arisugawa Takehito|Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Jingu|List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:11]] [[Category|Kehi-jingu}} * {{Official|http://kehijingu.jp}} *[http://www.turuga.org/en/kehijingu/kehijingu.html Tsuruga Tourism Association] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Tsuruga, Fukui]] [[Category:Echizen Province]] [[Category:8th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] }} [[sn:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingu]] [[sn:ehi Jingū]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

{{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Echizen Province|Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. In the Heian and [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[:en:Sado Province|Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''. In the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[:en:Yūki Hideyasu|Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]]on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Hokuriku Main Line|Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. {{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Echizen Province|Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] and the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], and [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[:en:Three Kingdoms of Korea|Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[:en:Empress Jito|Empress Jito]]. During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its Ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[:en:Nihon Kiryaku|Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[:en:Sado Province|Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]] during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[:en:Yūki Hideyasu|Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Ōno Domain|Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[:en:Matsuo Basho|Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[:en:Haiku|Haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st Rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]]on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Hokuriku Main Line|Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[:en:Arisugawa Takehito|Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Jingu|List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:11]] [[Category|Kehi-jingu}} * {{Official|http://kehijingu.jp}} *[http://www.turuga.org/en/kehijingu/kehijingu.html Tsuruga Tourism Association] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Tsuruga, Fukui]] [[Category:Echizen Province]] [[Category:8th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] }} [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[Category:Missing wikidata]]

The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]]on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Hokuriku Main Line|Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[:en:Arisugawa Takehito|Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Jingu|List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:11]] [[Category|Kehi-jingu}} * {{Official|http://kehijingu.jp}} *[http://www.turuga.org/en/kehijingu/kehijingu.html Tsuruga Tourism Association] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Tsuruga, Fukui]] [[Category:Echizen Province]] [[Category:8th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] }} [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[Category:Foreign language page not connected to wikidata]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Echizen Province|Echizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to Tsuruga during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]]'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]]'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] and the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], and [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and the Japanese conquest of the [[:en:Three Kingdoms of Korea|Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[:en:Empress Jito|Empress Jito]]. During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its Ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[:en:Nihon Kiryaku|Nihon Kiryaku]]'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo]] and [[:en:Sado Province|Sado Province]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]] during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[:en:Yūki Hideyasu|Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Ōno Domain|Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[:en:Matsuo Basho|Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[:en:Haiku|Haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st Rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]]on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Hokuriku Main Line|Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[:en:Arisugawa Takehito|Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Jingu|List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:11]] [[Category|Kehi-jingu}} * {{Official|http://kehijingu.jp}} *[http://www.turuga.org/en/kehijingu/kehijingu.html Tsuruga Tourism Association] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Tsuruga, Fukui]] [[Category:Echizen Province]] [[Category:8th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] }} [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[Category:Missing wikidata]]

氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], and even after defeat by the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]], still commanded territory with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 240,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''. The shrine supported the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]] during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]. In the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by [[:en:Yūki Hideyasu|Yūki Hideyasu]] in 1604. It also received donation from [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and by the various ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Ōno Domain|Ōno Domain]], but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[:en:Matsuo Basho|Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his [[:en:Haiku|Haiku]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st Rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]] ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|[Important Cultural Property]]. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]’s [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]]’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]]on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Hokuriku Main Line|Hokuriku Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[:en:Arisugawa Takehito|Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Jingu|List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:11]] [[Category|Kehi-jingu}} * {{Official|http://kehijingu.jp}} *[http://www.turuga.org/en/kehijingu/kehijingu.html Tsuruga Tourism Association] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Tsuruga, Fukui]] [[Category:Echizen Province]] [[Category:8th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] }} [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[Category:Jingus without interwikis]]

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shrine in Tsuruga, Fukui Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kehi Shrine
氣比神宮 | image = 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg | caption = Nakatorii of Kehi Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.6550|N|136.0747|E|source:wikidata|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Izasawake no mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 11-68 Akebono-chō, Tsuruga-shi, Fukui-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://kehijingu.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = Reitaisai (September 4th) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|{{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kehi Shrine'''|氣比神宮|Kehi Jingū}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in the Akebono-chō neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Tsuruga, Fukui|en|Tsuruga, Fukui|WD=Q847581|lt=Tsuruga}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Echizen Province|en|Echizen Province|WD=Q1047144|lt=Echizen Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 4.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is considered the chief guardian shrine of the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kehi Jingū are: *{{nihongo|'''Izasawake-no-mikoto'''|伊奢沙別命}}, the semi-legendary Prince Tsunuga Arashito, who traveled from his home in {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} to Tsuruga during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' he had horns coming from his head; the area was named “Tsunuga,” written, literally, “horn-deer,” and over time it became “Tsuruga.” *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai}}'''|仲哀天皇|Chūai-tennō}}, the semi-legendary 14th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}}'''|神功皇后|Jingū-kōgō}}, consort of Emperor Chūai *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}}'''|ヤマトタケルノミコト|Yamato Takeru no Mikoto}} *{{nihongo|'''Tama-hime no mikoto'''|玉姫命|}}, the daughter of Empress Jingū *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}'''|応神天皇|Ōjin-tennō}}, the 15th Emperor of Japan *{{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰}}, legendary hero-statesman ==History== The Kehi Jingū is located in the northeastern part of Tsuruga city in the central part of Fukui prefecture. Tsuruga has a good natural port, and was the gateway between the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] and the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}} and Asian continent since ancient times. Because of its location, the shine was given particular importance by the imperial court, and is mentioned frequently in ancient chronicles such as the "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihon Shoki]]", especially in connection to events connected with the semi-legendary {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}} and the Japanese conquest of the {{ill|Three Kingdoms of Korea|en|Three Kingdoms of Korea|WD=Q165292|lt=Three Kingdoms of Korea}}. The actual foundation of the shrine predates written history, and its first mention in national chronicles is from around 692 AD during the reign of [[:en:Empress Jito|Empress Jito]]. During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, imperial messengers were frequently dispatched to the shrine, and its Ranking within the court hierarchy steadily rose, as described in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', ''{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|en|Nihon Kiryaku|WD=Q1053542|lt=Nihon Kiryaku}}'' and other historical sources. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'', it is listed as a ''myōjin taisha'' and by the end of the Heian period was the ''ichinomiya'' of the province. In the Heian and {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the shrine commanded vast estates extending from Echizen to {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo}} and {{ill|Sado Province|en|Sado Province|WD=Q1063844|lt=Sado Province}}. The shrine sided with the [[Southern Court]] in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}, and even after defeat by the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}}, still commanded territory with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' of 240,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}''. The shrine supported the {{ill|Asakura clan|en|Asakura clan|WD=Q1772267|lt=Asakura clan}} during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} and was thus largely destroyed by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. In the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine's restored with a very modest 100 ''koku'' by {{ill|Yūki Hideyasu|en|Yūki Hideyasu|WD=Q1130929|lt=Yūki Hideyasu}} in 1604. It also received donation from {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} and by the various ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Ōno Domain|en|Ōno Domain|WD=Q10863325|lt=Ōno Domain}}, but never regained its former prosperity. In September of 1689, Kehi Jingu Shrine was visited by the famous poet [[:en:Matsuo Basho|Matsuo Basho]], and his visit is still commemorated with a statue and a stone monument inscribed with his {{ill|Haiku|en|Haiku|WD=Q37707|lt=Haiku}}.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, the shrine was rated as a{{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st Rank |官幣大社|Kanpei Taisha}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was mostly destroyed in 1945, during the [[Bombing of Tsuruga in World War II]] by American forces, and its {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}} ''[[Honden]]'' built in the Edo period by Yūki Hideyasu burned down. One surviving structure is the shrine's 11-meter-tall [[torii]] gate, which was built in 1902 and which is registered as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=[Important Cultural Property}}. It is the third largest wooden torii gate in Japan, alongside {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}’s [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and {{ill|Hiroshima|en|Hiroshima|WD=Q34664|lt=Hiroshima}}’s [[Itsukushima Shrine]]. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Tsuruga Station|en|Tsuruga Station|WD=Q5364670|lt=Tsuruga Station}}on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Hokuriku Main Line|en|Hokuriku Main Line|WD=Q1318747|lt=Hokuriku Main Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[:en:Arisugawa Takehito|Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Jingu|List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:11]] [[Category|Kehi-jingu}} * {{Official|http://kehijingu.jp}} *[http://www.turuga.org/en/kehijingu/kehijingu.html Tsuruga Tourism Association] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Tsuruga, Fukui]] [[Category:Echizen Province]] [[Category:8th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] }} [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[Category:Jingus without interwikis]]

The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Tsuruga Station|en|Tsuruga Station|WD=Q5364670|lt=Tsuruga Station}}on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Hokuriku Main Line|en|Hokuriku Main Line|WD=Q1318747|lt=Hokuriku Main Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kehi-jingu otorii-2.jpg|Ōtorii (ICP) Kehi-jingu honden-2.jpg|Honden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan08n.jpg|Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan05s3.jpg|Middle Torii and Outer Haiden 150228 Kehi-jingu Tsuruga Fukui pref Japan10n.jpg|Gate Kehi-jingu emaden.JPG|Ema Hall Kehi-jingu06s3200.jpg|former shrine offices ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Kehi Jingū Ō[[torii]]'''|気比神宮大鳥居||}}, Edo Period, 1645, wooden, painted vermillion. This torii gate has a height of 10.93 meters. The inscription is by Prince [[:en:Arisugawa Takehito|Arisugawa Takehito]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/817|title=気比神宮大鳥居〉|trans-title=Kehi Jingū Ōtorii |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Jingu|List of Jingu]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==External links== {{Commons Category:11]] [[Category|Kehi-jingu}} * {{Official|http://kehijingu.jp}} *[http://www.turuga.org/en/kehijingu/kehijingu.html Tsuruga Tourism Association] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Tsuruga, Fukui]] [[Category:Echizen Province]] [[Category:8th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] }} [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[en:Kehi Jingu]] [[Category:qq]] [[en:Kehi Jingū]] [[Category:Jingus without interwikis]]
